title: Directive on NCII Asset Categorization
subject: Framework for categorizing NCII assets by criticality, impact level, and dependency relationships
verificationStatus: not-verified-against-gazette — this instrument is a constructed representation of what such a directive might contain; it has not been verified against any officially published gazette instrument

provisions.title: Purpose and scope
provisions.requirement: This directive establishes the framework for the identification, categorization, and classification of assets constituting or supporting national critical information infrastructure. The categorization produced under this directive forms the basis for determining the applicable compliance tier under sector codes of practice, the depth of risk assessment and audit required, and the prioritization of vulnerability remediation and incident response activities.
provisions.explanation: Asset categorization is foundational to the entire compliance framework. The category assigned to each NCII asset determines the level of protection it requires, the stringency of controls in the applicable code of practice, and how urgently vulnerabilities must be patched. This directive ensures consistent categorization across all NCII sectors.

provisions.title: Categorization methodology
provisions.requirement: Each NCII entity shall categorize its national critical information infrastructure assets using the NACSA NCII Asset Criticality Matrix, which evaluates each asset against the following dimensions: (a) Service Impact — the severity and breadth of disruption to essential services if the asset is compromised or unavailable, measured on a five-point scale from Negligible to Catastrophic; (b) Data Sensitivity — the classification and volume of data processed, stored, or transmitted by the asset, including personal data, classified government information, and operationally sensitive data; (c) Interconnection Risk — the degree to which the asset is interconnected with other NCII assets, external networks, or shared services, and the potential for cascading failures; (d) Recovery Complexity — the time, effort, and resources required to restore the asset to full operational capability after a compromise; and (e) National Security Relevance — the direct or indirect contribution of the asset to national security, defence, foreign relations, or the ability of the Federal or State Governments to carry out their functions.
provisions.explanation: The five-dimension assessment ensures that criticality is evaluated holistically, not just by service availability. An asset might process limited data but be highly interconnected, creating cascading risk. Or it might be easy to recover but process classified government information. The multi-dimensional approach captures these nuances and prevents under-classification of assets that are critical in ways that a single metric would miss.

provisions.title: Criticality classifications
provisions.requirement: Based on the assessment under the NCII Asset Criticality Matrix, each asset shall be classified into one of four criticality levels: (a) Critical — assets whose compromise or unavailability would have a catastrophic or severe impact on the delivery of essential services, national security, or public safety, or would cause cascading failures across multiple NCII entities or sectors. Critical assets are subject to the most stringent controls under Tier 3 of sector codes of practice; (b) High — assets whose compromise or unavailability would have a major impact on essential service delivery or would significantly affect the entity's ability to fulfil its functions. High assets are subject to enhanced controls under Tier 2 of sector codes of practice; (c) Medium — assets whose compromise or unavailability would have a moderate impact on service delivery and can be compensated through alternative mechanisms within a reasonable timeframe; and (d) Low — supporting assets whose compromise or unavailability would have limited impact and which do not directly process, store, or transmit sensitive data.
provisions.explanation: The four-tier classification maps directly to the tiered compliance approach in sector codes of practice. Critical and High assets receive the most protection and fastest remediation timelines. The descriptions are written to enable consistent classification across sectors, while the NCII Asset Criticality Matrix provides the detailed scoring methodology.

provisions.title: Dependency mapping
provisions.requirement: Each NCII entity shall, as part of the categorization exercise, produce a dependency map identifying: (a) upstream dependencies — external systems, services, and components upon which the entity's NCII assets depend, including third-party cloud services, telecommunications links, power supply, and shared infrastructure; (b) downstream dependencies — other NCII entities, government services, or public services that depend on the entity's NCII assets; (c) intra-entity dependencies — internal dependencies between the entity's own NCII assets; and (d) shared component dependencies — common software, hardware, or service components used across multiple NCII assets that represent single points of failure. The dependency map shall be submitted to the Chief Executive and the relevant NCII sector lead together with the NCII Infrastructure Inventory under the NCII Entity Registration directive and updated annually or upon material change.
provisions.explanation: Understanding dependencies is essential for assessing cascading risk and coordinating incident response. If a telecommunications provider's NCII goes down, knowing which banking, energy, and government NCII depends on it enables coordinated response. The four categories of dependency ensure comprehensive coverage from external supply chains to internal single points of failure.

provisions.title: Initial categorization and review cycle
provisions.requirement: Every NCII entity shall complete its initial asset categorization within sixty (60) calendar days of registration under the NCII Entity Registration directive. The categorization shall be reviewed and updated: (a) at intervals not exceeding twelve (12) months; (b) within thirty (30) calendar days following any material change to the entity's NCII infrastructure, including the deployment of new systems, decommissioning of existing systems, significant changes to system architecture, or changes in the services supported by the infrastructure; and (c) within thirty (30) calendar days following a direction by the Chief Executive to review the categorization. The results of each categorization or review shall be submitted to the Chief Executive and the relevant NCII sector lead.
provisions.explanation: Asset categorization must be completed within 60 days of registration and kept current through annual reviews and event-triggered updates. This ensures the categorization reflects the actual state of the entity's infrastructure rather than becoming a static document that diverges from reality as systems change.

provisions.title: Chief Executive override
provisions.requirement: The Chief Executive may, by written notice to an NCII entity, direct the reclassification of any asset to a higher criticality level where the Chief Executive considers that: (a) the entity's self-assessment does not adequately reflect the asset's importance to national critical information infrastructure protection; (b) new information regarding cross-sector dependencies or national security relevance warrants a higher classification; or (c) an incident or threat assessment has revealed that the asset's compromise would have a more severe impact than the entity's categorization reflects. An entity directed to reclassify an asset shall implement the controls and measures corresponding to the new classification within sixty (60) calendar days of the direction, or such other period as the Chief Executive may specify.
provisions.explanation: While entities perform self-assessment, the Chief Executive retains the power to override classifications upward. This prevents entities from strategically under-classifying assets to avoid stricter controls. The override power is limited to upward reclassification only, ensuring it serves its protective purpose without being used to reduce security requirements.

provisions.title: Cross-sector categorization consistency
provisions.requirement: NACSA shall, in coordination with NCII sector leads, maintain the NCII Asset Criticality Matrix and ensure consistency in its application across all NCII sectors. NACSA shall: (a) publish guidance on the application of the Matrix, including worked examples for each NCII sector; (b) provide training to NCII entities and sector leads on categorization methodology; (c) conduct periodic cross-sector reviews to identify inconsistencies in categorization approaches; and (d) update the Matrix as necessary to reflect changes in the national threat landscape, technology environment, or NCII sector structure. Any update to the Matrix shall be communicated to all NCII entities and sector leads with a minimum of sixty (60) calendar days' notice before taking effect.
provisions.explanation: Consistent categorization across sectors is essential for fair and effective regulation. A High-criticality asset in the energy sector should represent a comparable risk level to a High-criticality asset in healthcare. NACSA ensures this consistency through sector-specific guidance, training, cross-sector reviews, and centralized Matrix maintenance. The 60-day notice for Matrix updates gives entities time to reassess their assets under new criteria.
